sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Maria Martin, a 76-year-old female, presents for follow-up.
She reports feeling "pretty good" and has been experiencing peripheral neuropathy in her feet, which she describes as feeling "like hell" all the time.
She also reports pain in her hips and upper legs.
She denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, coughing, chest pains, pressure, or tightness.
She reports being constipated and having a slow stream of urine, but states it is stable.
She has had cataract surgery, which has improved her vision, but is not satisfied with the new lens.
She has not had a fever, chills, or nausea.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, and vascular disease in her legs.
She has been taking glimepiride 2mg twice a day, metoprolol 50mg once a day, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20/25mg once a day, amlodipine 10mg once a day, and atorvastatin.
She is a smoker.
Review of systems:
The patient denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, coughing, chest pains, pressure, or tightness.
She reports constipation and a slow stream of urine, but states it is stable.
Current medications:
Glimepiride 2mg twice a day
Metoprolol 50mg once a day
Lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20/25mg once a day
Amlodipine 10mg once a day
Atorvastatin
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 140/60s (rechecked)
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
Carotids are normal.
Pulses are palpable on the dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial.
Extremities are without edema.
Mildly dry skin, no skin breakdown.
Mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes.
Thickened nails.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has poorly controlled diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, and vascular disease in her legs.
She has peripheral neuropathy in her feet and pain in her hips and upper legs.
Problem list: Diabetes mellitus; hypertension; hypercholesterolemia; peripheral vascular disease; peripheral neuropathy; constipation.
Plan:
I want the patient to increase her glimepiride to 4mg twice a day and to take two of the 2mg pills until she gets the new prescription.
I also want her to continue taking metformin.
I will recheck her blood pressure and check her kidney function.
I will continue her on the high dose of atorvastatin to protect her arteries.
I will get the labs today and see her back in 4 months.
Follow-up:
We will see the patient back in 4 months.
